narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80 and LSAT score of 26. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 95.9% chance that you would fail it—far higher than what it sees in most applicants. This high predicted risk is why your application was rejected; the school uses this model to prioritize students likely to succeed on the bar and graduate successfully. The three biggest factors pushing the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 school decile ranking, in that order of influence. Your Year 3 decile of 1—well below the typical student's 5.5—had the strongest negative impact, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the bottom tier compared to peers, which strongly correlates with bar struggles. Similarly, your LSAT score of 26, much lower than the average of about 37, reinforced this risk by suggesting challenges with the analytical skills needed for law school and the bar. Even your Year 1 decile of 1, again far under the average around 5.7, added to the concern, as early low rankings often predict ongoing difficulties. While your full-time status aligns with most students and your GPA was somewhat closer to average, these top issues overshadowed them. Compared to typical applicants who pass the bar, you're in the lowest deciles for those early and later grades, with an LSAT that puts you well below the pack. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your high school or undergrad record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and targeting schools with strong support for first-generation or underrepresented students like yourself. Building a standout personal statement highlighting your resilience could also help in reapplying, perhaps to programs with holistic reviews that weigh your unique background more favorably. With targeted improvements, you can shift the odds in your favor next time.
